meta.model: claude-opus-4-8 (Claude Code weekly review, injected call_fn)

digests.narrative:
The sold-out story is now triple-sourced and he treats the market's refusal to price it as the opportunity. POET's own investor relations confirmed the big optical makers — LITE, COHR — are completely sold out for the next two years and likely into 2029 for photonics; Innolight's IR revised 800G demand sharply upward ("demand has not contracted; instead, 800G demand has increased significantly") with the shortage list broadening beyond EML/CW chips to PCBs and module materials; and Morgan Stanley's CPO note independently named SIVE, COHR, and LITE the laser-supply leaders — the sell-side validation of his three-leader OSINT framing, with SIVE a ~$1.5B company placed beside two $60B+ names.

He sharpened the internal differentiation: AAOI is a pluggables business (no CPO design wins, CEO says commercial timing is 'TBD') that benefits from the 800G revision and formally commenced its 400k sqft Texas buildout; MACOM has fallen off the CPO laser map. Rising silicon-photonics mix at Innolight's customers shifts demand from EML toward CW lasers — the SIVE/JBL side of the ledger. His closing frame for the week's selloff: nothing is fundamentally wrong, corrections flush leverage, and lasers sold out into 2029 while the market sells laser companies is a disconnect, not a warning.
keyPoints: Lasers sold out into 2029 (LITE/COHR) now confirmed by three independent channels
Morgan Stanley names SIVE, COHR, LITE the CPO laser-supply leaders — sell-side validates his trio
Innolight revises 800G demand sharply upward; shortage list broadens beyond EML/CW
AAOI clarified as a pluggables (not CPO) story; Texas 400k sqft buildout commenced
Rising SiPh mix shifts demand from EML toward CW lasers (SIVE/JBL side)
He reads the sector selloff as leverage flush against improving fundamentals

digests.narrative:
The upcycle hardened from price forecasts into contracts — and the market sold it anyway, which is now his central point. DRAM contract prices are set for 20%+ hikes next quarter, SLC NAND is forecast up 120–170% for H2 2026 per TrendForce, and general NAND flash has already run ~5x this year on RAG/inference demand. The demand side is locking in long-term: MU has signed 16+ LTAs, SNDK signed directly with META, and Samsung keeps projecting further DRAM hikes after becoming the most profitable company in the world. His refrain: 'Market: proceeds to sell off memory.'

The AEHR print gave the theme a picks-and-shovels kicker — a completed wafer-level burn-in benchmark with a 'global leader in NAND flash' (he reads SanDisk) now moving toward a development agreement around HBM/NAND. On SLC NAND specifically he notes the cleaner beneficiaries are smaller Asian players (Winbond, Macronix, SkyHigh via Puya) since Micron's $1T scale dwarfs the SLC TAM.
keyPoints: DRAM 20%+ contract hikes next quarter; SLC NAND forecast +120–170% H2 2026
Demand locked long-term: MU 16+ LTAs, SNDK–META agreement, NAND ~5x this year
Market sells memory names into confirmed hikes — the disconnect he's trading
AEHR NAND burn-in benchmark (likely SanDisk) opens an HBM/NAND qualification path
SLC NAND upside accrues to smaller players (Winbond, Macronix) more than MU

digests.narrative:
The silicon-photonics foundry layer got externally mapped this week. Morgan Stanley's CPO note placed TSM, GFS, and TSEM as the silicon-photonics foundry capacity beneath the CPO laser leaders — the same "platform beneath his photonics book" frame he's held, now with a sell-side stamp. Tower Semi (TSEM) reinforced it with a concrete capacity announcement: a Japanese-government-supported expansion of 300mm silicon-photonics, SiGe, and advanced-packaging capacity, targeting $1.2B net profit and $3.6B revenue in 2028 (~23x 2028 forward P/E at a ~$28B market cap) — a fab putting capex behind the SiPho ramp rather than just being named in a note.

The durable specialty-foundry idea is unchanged and still the strongest standalone fab-layer thesis: XFAB as the cheapest specialty foundry with a free photonixfab silicon-photonics option, GlobalFoundries as the SCALE reference-laser platform where AMD's CPO reportedly routes, and the equipment names (ASML/AMAT/LRCX/KLAC) as the unavoidable layer beneath the whole stack. His cross-sector frame holds: when the tape sells the fab/equipment complex on macro leverage, sold-out SiPho and advanced-packaging capacity means the order books didn't move with the price.
keyPoints: Morgan Stanley mapped TSM/GFS/TSEM as the silicon-photonics foundry capacity beneath the CPO laser leaders — sell-side stamp on his platform frame
Tower Semi (TSEM): Japan-backed 300mm SiPho/SiGe/advanced-packaging expansion, targeting $3.6B revenue / $1.2B net profit in 2028
Capacity is being put behind the SiPho ramp, not just named — capex validation of the foundry-layer thesis
Durable idea intact: XFAB cheapest specialty foundry (+ free photonixfab option); GFS the SCALE reference-laser platform for AMD's CPO
Equipment names (ASML/AMAT/LRCX/KLAC) remain the unavoidable layer — they trade with macro routs even when order books don't move

digests.narrative: Customer diversification — the strength he's always cited for NBIS — delivered its proof: a $1B+ compute agreement with Reflection AI for GB300 access through 2029. The stock fell ~5% on the news, and this time he supplies the caveat himself: Reflection (which also signed multi-billion deals on the SpaceX side) reminds him of OpenAI-style counterparties that may not yet have the funds to execute their commitments, unlike META or MSFT. Long-term positive, he says, but contract quality now shares the stage with contract count.
keyPoints: NBIS signs $1B+ GB300 compute deal with Reflection AI through 2029
Customer diversification thesis delivering — but stock fell on the news
He flags counterparty funding risk himself (the OpenAI-commitment pattern)
Contract quality (fundable counterparties) now matters as much as volume

digests.narrative:
The theme's sleeper woke up violently: AEHR rallied +36% on earnings, guiding FY2027 to $130–150M (160–200% growth) on $60.7M Q4 bookings and a $100M+ effective backlog. His core observation is breadth — wafer-level burn-in qualification tracks now span AI processors (lead customer significantly ramping), a benchmark 'major supplier of AI accelerators, CPUs and network processors' that exceeded expectations, silicon photonics (already ramping), and a NAND flash leader moving toward an HBM/NAND development agreement. He argues the guide understates 2027 if benchmarks convert to high-volume manufacturing, while flagging the violence of the ride ($116 → $60 → $94 within a month).

The quieter thread is his Nippon Chemical rabbit hole: NCI's red phosphorus (with Rasa, #1 globally) feeds AXTI's InP substrates, its phosphine gas feeds Entegris into the Kioxia/MU/SK Hynix fabs, and its barium chemistry feeds the MLCC bottleneck via TDK/Murata — 'one of the most important companies in the world' at a tiny valuation, though Japanese pricing culture caps how much of that chokepoint value ever reaches the P&L.
keyPoints: AEHR +36% on earnings: FY27 guide $130–150M (160–200% growth), $100M+ backlog
Burn-in qualification spans AI processors, silicon photonics, and NAND/HBM
He thinks the guide understates 2027 if benchmark customers convert to HVM
Nippon Chemical deep-dive: red phosphorus → InP; phosphine → memory fabs; barium → MLCC
Japanese pricing culture caps chokepoint value capture — thesis caveat he owns

digests.narrative: Two supply-side breadcrumbs this week, still no dedicated networking thesis. His semi recap flags Starlink Gen 3 scaling to 100,000 units (10x the previous generation), creating possible capacity constraints for switch suppliers into CCL, and PCB supply shortages projected to persist until 2028 — component inflation already forcing ODMs into conservative H2 shipment guides. The Marvell thread got indirect help: Morgan Stanley's CPO note slots Marvell/Celestial among the optical-engine players, the first sell-side corroboration of his half-serious NVDA-backs-the-ASIC-challengers theory — which he still hasn't converted into a numbers-driven thesis.
keyPoints: Starlink Gen 3 scaling 10x — possible switch/CCL supplier capacity constraints
PCB shortages projected to persist until 2028; ODMs already guiding conservatively
MS places Marvell/Celestial in CPO optical engines — first corroboration of the MRVL angle
Still no dedicated, numbers-driven networking thesis from him

digests.narrative: First substantive datapoint for the theme: per his Morgan Stanley recap, Corning's 'glass bridge' has potential in the CPO stack but will struggle to displace incumbent FAU solutions (like FOCI's) in the short term — and separately Largan Precision secured its first CPO FAU order with mass production slated for mid-next-year, confirming where that socket currently sits. Still no dedicated glass thesis from him; the theme remains a tracked candidate for the glass-core packaging wave rather than an active position.
keyPoints: MS: GLW glass bridge has potential but hard to displace FAU incumbents near-term
Largan's first CPO FAU order confirms the incumbent socket
Still no dedicated glass-substrate thesis — tracked, not owned

digests.narrative:
The theme got its first institutional scaffolding: an IBK Research report on Boston Dynamics value chains that he mostly uses as a measuring stick. He rejects their linear volume ramp (+10K units/year) for an S-curve — his own guess is 90–140K units by 2030 versus IBK's 30K — anchored on Boston Dynamics' 30K capacity target for 2028 and China already collectively shipping ~100K by end-2026. The report's most useful claim, in his view: actuator capacity is the cleanest forward indicator of the ramp (~310K actuators per 10K robots), making actuator output, yields and ASPs the thing to track through 2028–2030.

TSLA supplied the first hard production datapoint of the cycle: procurement guidelines requiring suppliers to hit 1,000 Optimus gen-3 units/week by September, doubling to 2,000–2,500 by year end. His conviction expression is still Agility via CCXI ('own as much % as possible' — maximum book-talking), with the IBK landscape placing Agility among the US leaders against Unitree/Figure/Apptronik and the Chinese volume machine.
keyPoints: IBK/Boston Dynamics report gives the theme institutional valuation scaffolding
He rejects linear ramps: his 2030 guess is 90–140K units vs IBK's 30K
Actuator capacity (~310K per 10K robots) is the leading indicator to track
TSLA Optimus gen-3 supplier mandates: 1,000/week by Sept, 2,000–2,500 by year end
Agility (via CCXI) remains his conviction pick — with heavy book-talking discount

digests.narrative:
Jensen Huang escalated from company statements to personal, on-the-record denial: in Tokyo he called the delay reports 'not true — Vera Rubin is already in production. Giant amounts of production incoming.' That's the second rebuttal in a week (after debunking the Rubin-Ultra-to-2028 rumor and reframing the Kyber change as architectural optimization). The analyst sides with Nvidia on timelines — they have the best visibility into their own supply chain — while noting the rumor cycle already did real damage to supply-chain stock prices, and calling out the growing pattern of overreaching claims spun from technical nuance for viewcount.

Around the edges: a frontier lab that leaned on internal ASICs has reportedly shifted ~50% of its compute back to Nvidia, DeepSeek and Zhipu are building custom ASICs to route around it (expected), and Nvidia hosts a CPO strategy conference with NTT on July 24 — a dated catalyst for the optics complex.
keyPoints: Jensen in Tokyo: 'Vera Rubin is already in production' — flat denial of delay reports
Second rebuttal in a week; he trusts NVDA's visibility on its own timelines
Rumor cycle already damaged supplier stock prices — narrative risk is the real risk
Frontier lab reportedly moved ~50% of compute from internal ASICs back to NVDA
NVDA/NTT CPO conference July 24 — dated catalyst for the optics complex

digests.narrative:
A possible fifth capex payer entered his frame: The Information reports AAPL is shopping for AI chip companies, which he reads as Apple learning the lesson GOOGL taught the industry when it cut Meta off — own your infrastructure or someone else rations it. If Apple follows M&A with a real buildout, that's an unexpected, unpriced demand wave across foundries, optics, and memory. No leaked targets yet; he's explicit this is scenario, not fact.

Meanwhile META keeps converting narrative into supply-chain fact: the Iris chip enters mass production in September via Broadcom and TSMC, compute doubles to 14GW by 2027, and its memory LTAs (Samsung, SanDisk) are underwriting the memory upcycle. His standing frame holds — hyperscaler capex plans sit at the high end of projections while the market keeps treating each confirmation as sell-the-news.
keyPoints: AAPL reportedly shopping for AI chip companies — a potential unpriced fifth capex wave
His read: Apple learned from Google cutting Meta off — self-reliance beats renting
META Iris chip to mass production in September via AVGO/TSMC; 14GW compute by 2027
Meta's memory LTAs (Samsung, SNDK) directly underwrite the memory upcycle
Hyperscaler capex at the high end of projections; market keeps selling the news
